message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="marty-cagan" color="blue" summary="Nav concerns addressed by incremental build order and empty states">
Good follow-up, Steve. Two responses:

**On phased navigation**: The team has already reached 9/9 consensus that all 8 user stories (including Waiting For and Someday/Maybe) are Must Have for V1. So the final nav will have all the GTD lists. However, your concern about 6+ nav items is valid from a cognitive load perspective. Here's how the implementation order addresses it naturally:

The implementation order is incremental (13 steps). Navigation starts with just the skeleton (step 1), then Inbox (step 2), then Next Actions (step 4), then Projects (step 6), then Waiting For (step 8), then Someday/Maybe (step 10), then Weekly Review (step 12). Each nav item appears when its feature ships. So during development, the nav IS progressive -- it grows as the system grows. By the time all 6 items are in the nav, the user has been using the system long enough to understand each one.

For a brand-new user encountering the fully-built V1 for the first time, the empty states (which we've now added to every story) serve as the progressive disclosure. They open Waiting For, see "Nothing pending from others. When you delegate something, add it here." -- and they instantly understand the list's purpose without reading a manual.

6 nav items is within the standard range for web apps. Email clients have more. The labels are self-explanatory. I'm confident this passes the Trunk Test.

**On the badge pattern**: Already in scope. US-GTD-8 includes "Inbox (with count)" in the navigation. The count is informational, not alarming (per our "calm over urgent" UX principle). Your email inbox analogy is exactly right.

**On the Trunk Test**: Already added to the UX Principles section (Section 7 of the discovery doc) as Principle #7. Every page must answer: Where am I? What are my options? How do I get back? The acceptance criteria for US-GTD-8 include `aria-current="page"` on the active nav link, which covers both visual and screen reader "where am I" indicators.

Your navigation instincts are sound. The documents already address these concerns. Thank you for reinforcing them.
</teammate-message>

content.text: Marty has addressed all my navigation concerns. The incremental build order naturally provides the progressive disclosure I was worried about, and the empty states cover first-time users encountering the fully-built system. No need to respond -- we're fully aligned.
